content.text: A 'Resource' is anything available in our environment that can be used to satisfy our needs, provided it is technologically accessible, economically feasible, and culturally acceptable.

content.title: Human Role
content.text: Human beings are essential components of resources. They interact with nature through technology and create institutions to accelerate economic development.

items: On the basis of Origin: Biotic (biosphere/life) and Abiotic (non-living things).
On the basis of Exhaustibility: Renewable (can be replenished like solar/wind) and Non-Renewable (take millions of years like fossil fuels).
On the basis of Ownership: Individual, Community, National (roads/canals), and International (beyond 200 nautical miles of EEZ).
On the basis of Status of Development: Potential (Gujarat solar), Developed (Stock/Reserves).

content.text: Indiscriminate use of resources has led to depletion, accumulation in few hands, and global ecological crises (Global warming, ozone depletion).

content.title: Rio Earth Summit 1992
content.text: More than 100 heads of states met in Brazil to address environmental protection. They adopted Agenda 21 for achieving global sustainable development in the 21st century.

content.title: Planning Steps:
items: Identification and inventory across regions (surveying/mapping).
Evolving planning structure with technology, skill, and institutions.
Matching resource plans with national development plans.

content.text: Conservation Focus: Gandhiji stated, 'There is enough for everybody’s need and not for any body’s greed.' He blamed greedy individuals and mass production technology for resource depletion.

notes.section_title: Land Resources and Land Use in India

rows: Reserves of minerals, fossil fuels, forests

content.text: Net Sown Area (NSA) in India is about 54% of the total reporting area. Forest area (23.4%) is far lower than the desired 33% outlined in the National Forest Policy (1952).

content.title: Causes of Degradation:
items: Mining: Scars left in Jharkhand, Chhattisgarh, Odisha.
Overgrazing: Gujarat, Rajasthan, Maharashtra.
Over-irrigation: Punjab, Haryana (due to water logging/salinity).
Industrial Waste: Effluents as major source of pollution.

content.text: Conservation: Shelter belts, stabilization of sand dunes (thorny bushes in arid areas), and proper discharge of industrial waste.

content.title: Soil Types in India:
items: Alluvial Soil: Most widely spread and fertile. Classified as Bangar (old - more kanker) and Khadar (new - more fine/fertile). Ideal for Sugarcane, Paddy, Wheat.
Black Soil (Regur): Ideal for cotton. Deccan trap (Basalt) region. Fine clayey, holds moisture, rich in Ca/Mg/Potash, poor in Phosphorous.
Red and Yellow Soil: Develops on crystalline igneous rocks. Red due to iron diffusion; yellow in hydrated form.
Laterite Soil: Derived from Latin 'later' (brick). Result of intense leaching under heavy rain. Suitable for tea/coffee/cashew (TN, Kerala).
Arid Soil: Red to brown, sandy texture, saline. High kankar restricts water infiltration. Cultivable with irrigation (Rajasthan).
Forest Soil: Hilly/Mountainous areas. Texture varies with environment (loamy in valleys, coarse in upper slopes). Humus poor in snow-covered areas.

content.title: Erosion Types:
items: Gully Erosion: Running water cuts deep channels; land becomes 'badland' (e.g., Ravines in Chambal).
Sheet Erosion: Topsoil washed away over large areas.
Wind Erosion: Blowing of loose soil off flat land.

content.title: Conservation Methods:
items: Contour Ploughing: Decelerates flow of water along slopes.
Terrace Farming: Restricted erosion in Himalayas.
Strip Cropping: Strips of grass left to grow between crops to break wind force.
Shelter Belts: Lines of trees to stabilize sand dunes in western India.

glossary.definition: Development that takes place without damaging the environment and does not compromise with the needs of future generations.
The physical extent of land on which crops are sown and harvested at least once in an agricultural year.
Bangar is old alluvial soil with more kanker nodules; Khadar is new alluvial soil with more fine particles and higher fertility.
Soil erosion caused by running water cutting through clayey soils making deep channels; makes land unfit for cultivation (Badland).
Rows of trees planted to create shelter and break the force of the wind, particularly to stabilize sand dunes.

summary_points: Resources must be technologically accessible, economically feasible, and culturally acceptable to be utilized.
Resource planning is essential for sustainable existence, guided by principles like Agenda 21 and the Brundtland Report.
Land use in India is dominated by plains (43%), but forest cover remains below the target of 33%.
Different geographical regions require specific conservation: shelter belts for deserts, water treatment for industrial zones, and terrace farming for hills.
Soil classification (Alluvial to Arid) highlights the diverse agricultural potential and conservation needs across India.
